description: In-depth guide to dynamic allocation using new and delete. Covers single objects, arrays, memory leaks, dangling pointers, and best practices. No smart pointers yet.

sections.title: Why Dynamic Allocation?
sections.definition: Static allocation (int x;) happens at compile time on the stack. Dynamic allocation happens at runtime on the heap, allowing variable size and lifetime control beyond the scope.

sections.title: new and delete for Single Objects
sections.definition: Use new to allocate one object on the heap. Returns a pointer. Use delete to free it. If you forget delete, memory leaks.

sections.title: new and delete for Arrays
sections.definition: Use new[] to allocate an array on heap. Returns pointer to first element. Use delete[] to free. Must match new[] with delete[] (not delete).

sections.title: Dynamic Allocation of Objects
sections.definition: new calls constructor, delete calls destructor. Works with classes and structs.

sections.title: Memory Leaks
sections.definition: A memory leak occurs when you allocate memory with new but never delete it. The memory remains allocated until program ends, causing waste and eventual crash if repeated.

sections.title: Dangling Pointers
sections.definition: After delete, the pointer still holds the address of freed memory. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior (crash, corruption).

sections.title: Double Delete (Undefined Behavior)
sections.definition: Deleting the same memory twice causes undefined behavior (usually crash). Avoid by setting pointers to nullptr after delete.

sections.title: Dynamic Allocation for 2D Arrays
sections.definition: Two common ways: array of pointers (each row separately) or a single flat array with manual index calculation.

sections.title: new and delete vs malloc/free
sections.definition: C++ uses new/delete because they call constructors/destructors. malloc/free from C only allocate raw memory. Never mix them.

sections.title: nothrow new
sections.definition: By default, new throws std::bad_alloc on failure. Use nothrow version to return nullptr instead.

sections.title: Placement new
sections.definition: Construct an object at a pre-allocated memory location. Used in custom memory management, pools, and embedded systems.
